verdict_reasoning: EREG is a secreted EGF-family ligand canonically released into solution as a soluble growth factor. However, it is encoded as a single-pass transmembrane proligand that undergoes ectodomain shedding by sheddases (ADAM10, ADAM17). The TM precursor stage is transient but real — the protein transiently anchors to the PM before regulated proteolytic release of the soluble ectodomain. This places it in the `dual_localization` / `contextual` bucket: the membrane-anchored precursor is a documented biological entity on the cell surface (capable of juxtacrine signaling), but the predominant detected form is the shed soluble ligand. Cell-surface-directed binders (antibodies, CAR-T systems) targeting the precursor or competitively blocking shedding would engage this transient surface pool, establishing at least contextual accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The stability and residence time of the TM precursor on the cell surface is not fully quantified in the provided NCBI summary. If the precursor is extremely short-lived (seconds to minutes before constitutive shedding), the contextual verdict might be borderline; however, the canonical EGF-family shedding-ligand architecture — where the TM stage feeds regulated release of an active soluble form — is well-established for EREG, warranting `contextual` / `dual_localization` over `secreted_only`.
